section.title: Introduction
section.para: This article describes how to assign an &nvidia; GPU graphics card on the host machine to a virtualized guest.

listitem.para: GPU pass-through is supported on the &x86-64; architecture only.
The host operating system needs to be SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 16 or newer.
This article provides instructions for GPU pass-through with &nvidia; cards, including Tesla, Data Center, and RTX series, and is meant for GPU computation purposes only.
For optimal CUDA compute performance with System-Allocated Memory (SAM) migration, kernel 6.12 or newer is recommended to include required HMM fixes.
To manage the host system, you need an additional display card on the host that you can use when configuring the GPU pass-through, or a functional SSH environment.

step.para: Verify that the host operating system is SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 16 or newer:
step.screen: &prompt.user;cat /etc/issue Welcome to SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 16.1 (x86_64) - Kernel \r (\l).

step.para: Verify that the host supports VT-d technology and that it is already enabled in the firmware settings:
step.screen: &prompt.user;dmesg | grep -e "Directed I/O" [ 12.819760] DMAR: Intel(R) Virtualization Technology for Directed I/O
step.para: If VT-d is not enabled in the firmware, enable it and reboot the host.
Verify that the host has an extra GPU or VGA card:
step.screen: &prompt.user;lspci | grep -i "vga" 07:00.0 VGA compatible controller: Matrox Electronics Systems Ltd. \ MGA G200e [Pilot] ServerEngines (SEP1) (rev 05)
step.para: With a Tesla V100 card:
step.screen: &prompt.user;lspci | grep -i nvidia 03:00.0 3D controller: NVIDIA Corporation GV100 [Tesla V100 PCIe] (rev a1)
step.para: With an A100 card:
step.screen: &prompt.user;lspci | grep -i nvidia 03:00.0 3D controller: NVIDIA Corporation A100-SXM4-40GB (rev a1)
step.para: With an L4 card:
step.screen: &prompt.user;lspci | grep -i nvidia 03:00.0 3D controller: NVIDIA Corporation L4 (rev a1)

section.title: Enable IOMMU
section.para: IOMMU is disabled by default. You need to enable it at boot time in the /etc/default/grub configuration file.

step.para: For Intel-based hosts:
For AMD-based hosts:
When you save the modified /etc/default/grub file, re-generate the main &grub; configuration file /boot/grub2/grub.cfg:
Reboot the host and verify that IOMMU is enabled:

section.title: Blacklist the Nouveau driver
section.para: To assign the &nvidia; card to a VM guest, we need to prevent the host OS from loading the built-in nouveau driver for &nvidia; GPUs. Create the file /etc/modprobe.d/60-blacklist-nouveau.conf with the following content:

section.title: Configure VFIO and isolate the GPU used for pass-through

step.para: Find the card vendor and model IDs. Use the bus number identified in the host verification step, for example, 03:00.0:
step.screen: &prompt.user;lspci -nn | grep 03:00.0 03:00.0 3D controller [0302]: NVIDIA Corporation GV100 [Tesla V100 PCIe] [10de:1db4] (rev a1)

step.para: Create the file /etc/modprobe.d/vfio.conf with the following content:

note.para: Verify that your card does not need an extra ids= parameter. For certain cards, you must specify the audio device too, therefore device's ID must also be added to the list, otherwise you cannot use the card.

section.title: Load the VFIO driver
section.para: There are three ways you can load the VFIO driver.

note.para: Kernel 6.12 introduces IOMMUFD as the new user API for VFIO. The legacy vfio_iommu_type1 container/group model is still supported but gradually being superseded. For libvirt-based passthrough (virt-manager, virt-install), no changes are required as libvirt abstracts the VFIO interface.

section.title: Including the driver in the initrd file

step.para: Create the file /etc/dracut.conf.d/gpu-passthrough.conf and add the following content (mind the leading whitespace):
Re-generate the initrd file:

section.title: Adding the driver to the list of auto-loaded modules
section.para: Create the file /etc/modules-load.d/vfio-pci.conf and add the following content:

section.title: Loading the driver manually
section.para: To load the driver manually at runtime, execute the following command:

section.title: Disable MSR for &mswin; guests
section.para: For &mswin; guests, we recommend disabling MSR (model-specific register) to avoid the guest crashing. Create the file /etc/modprobe.d/kvm.conf and add the following content:

section.title: Install UEFI firmware
section.para: For proper GPU pass-through functionality, the host needs to boot using UEFI firmware (that is, not using a legacy-style BIOS boot sequence). Install the qemu-ovmf package if not already installed:

section.title: Reboot the host machine
section.para: For most of the changes in the above steps to take effect, you need to reboot the host machine:

section.title: Configuring the guest
section.para: This section describes how to configure the guest virtual machine so that it can use the host's &nvidia; GPU. Use virt-manager or virt-install to install the guest VM. Find more details in the KVM installation guide.

section.title: Requirements for the guest configuration
section.para: During the guest VM installation, select Customize configuration before install and configure the following devices:

listitem.para: Use Q35 chipset for proper PCIe passthrough with modern GPUs.
Install the guest VM using UEFI firmware.
Find more information in the libvirt configuration documentation.
Add the host PCI device (03:00.0 in our example) to the guest. Find more information in the PCI device configuration documentation.
For the best performance, we recommend using virtio drivers for the network card and storage.

step.para: Download the driver RPM package from the official &nvidia; driver download page at .
Install the downloaded RPM package:
Refresh repositories and install cuda-drivers. This step is different for non-&suse; distributions:
Reboot the guest VM:

note.para: CUDA 13.3 requires driver version 610.43.02 or newer. The minimum driver version for CUDA 13.x is 580.

step.para: Because the installer needs to compile the &nvidia; driver modules, install the gcc-c++ and kernel-devel packages.

Disable Secure Boot on the guest, because &nvidia;'s driver modules are unsigned. On &suse; distributions, disable Secure Boot in the UEFI firmware setup or use mokutil to enroll a Machine Owner Key. Find more information in the UEFI Secure Boot documentation.
Download the driver installation script from , make it executable and run it to complete the driver installation:

Download CUDA drivers from
and install following the on-screen instructions.

note.title: Display issues
note.para: After you have installed the &nvidia; drivers, the virt-manager display loses its connection to the guest OS. To access the guest VM, you must either login via ssh, change to the console interface, or install a dedicated VNC server in the guest. To avoid a flickering screen, stop and disable the display manager:

step.para: Verify the installation with the NVIDIA System Management Interface:
Compile and run a CUDA sample. For example, with the deviceQuery sample:

important.para: Before you install the &nvidia; drivers, you need to hide the hypervisor from the drivers by using the <hidden state='on'/> directive in the guest's &libvirt; definition, for example:

step.para: Download and install the &nvidia; driver from .
Download and install the CUDA toolkit from .
Find several &nvidia; demo samples in the directory Program Files\Nvidia GPU Computing Toolkit\CUDA\v12.x\extras\demo_suite on the guest.

note.para: Starting with CUDA 13.1, the Windows display driver is no longer bundled with the CUDA Toolkit. You must download and install the appropriate NVIDIA driver separately from the official NVIDIA driver download page.
